data.title: The RealReal, Inc. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by The RealReal, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Royalty Pharma: Keep Delivering, Buy Confirmed
data.description:
Royalty Pharma plc (NASDAQ:RPRX) has reported its latest results. The company's performance strongly surpassed our expectations, reinforcing our view that it is well-positioned for an even brighter future.

For new readers, Royalty Pharma is the world’s largest acquirer of biopharmaceutical royalties by transaction value. Its model is straightforward, though it requires some pharma and finance knowledge: the company buys royalties that generate payments tied directly to the top‑line sales of leading therapies. Since our strong buy recommendation (Massive Opportunity Ahead, Nov 2024), RP's shares have risen by almost 48% (including the dividend payment, Fig. 1).

Aside from a valuation gap, our buy was backed by 1) RP's ability to generate FCF, 2) sound capital allocation priorities (M&A first, dividend increase year-on-year, and a $3 billion buyback), and 3) a corporate structure simplification. On the downside, we also highlighted the Vertex franchise development.

Fig 1

Q2 Results and Our Positive Take

Royalty Pharma’s revenue stream is straightforward. Royalty receipts rose 11% to $672 million (Fig. 2), with an adjusted EBITDA increase of 13%. In this quarter, the company also reported a sales milestone of $56 million. For the RP business nature (with no operating costs), this led to an EPS beat and a higher cash flow generation. Milestone and other contractual payments are excluded from our guidance due to these inflows' limited visibility and inherent unpredictability.

On the core business (Fig. 2), RP reported higher royalties from Evrysdi (+32%), Tremfya (+24%), and Cabometyx/Cometriq (+22%). In number, the Q2 company's portfolio cash flow increased by 12% to $562 million.

Source: Royalty Pharma Q2 press release, Fig 2

Why are we positive?

Our investment thesis on Royalty Pharma is playing out as anticipated, and we can confidently check the boxes on our key investment pillars. We now highlight two further upside catalysts:

  1. RP communicated the RP Management acquisition. This will combine the RP portfolio with manager IP, leading to a unique enterprise. Thus reducing the cost structure. In the FY 2025 guidance, the company is already reporting 50/100 basis points of savings in professional costs (Fig. 3);
  2. We can report solid execution with outstanding royalty growth year-on-year. While growth seems to moderate in %, the company is executing billion‑dollar transactions, which should underpin future earnings expansion and provide greater visibility. The company communicated a new funding agreement with Revolution Medicines (Fig. 4) for an initial value of $1.25 billion. Daraxonrasib is a multi-selective inhibitor in Phase III development and cures mutant pancreatic cancer and non-small cell lung cancer. This is a multi-billion-dollar blockbuster (Fig. 5) with potential royalties for the next 15 years;
  3. Aside from the new royalties, the company continues to remunerate its shareholders based on. Aside from the DPS increase, the company bought back 8 million shares for a total value of $277 million. There was a $1 billion repurchase in H1, with a $3 billion target. Thanks to MorphoSys' disposal and a strong FCF, the company still has $632 in cash.

On the new upside: 1) the company raised its full-year guidance (this is also supported by Milestones' contractual agreement with almost $110 million in sales in 2025), and the portfolio receipts are expected to increase by at least +2.5% (Fi. 3) and 2) during the earnings call, the CEO mentioned upside from Chinese royalties.

Although not directly tied to Royalty Pharma, one of the latest additions to our portfolio is an investment in Arrivent Biopharma. The company completed an exclusive licensing agreement with Shanghai Allist Pharmaceuticals to sell its future product worldwide except in China.

Royalty and licensing deals in China typically involve lower upfront costs and higher royalty rates, which could present an opportunity for additional upside for Royalty Pharma.

Source: Royalty Pharma Q2 ppt - Fig 3

Fig 4

Fig 5

Earnings Changes and Valuation

In our last analysis, we increased our portfolio receipts growth to 9% with top-line sales of $3.1 billion. This estimate is now aligned with RP's latest updated guidance. On the EBITDA, we reported RP has a limited cost structure, but this year, they incorporated RP Management, and with MorphoSys sales, RP recorded $70 million of one-off expenses, so our EBITDA moved down from $2.85 billion to $2.78 billion. With no D&A, our interest payment also increased to $275 million. With no corporate tax, our earnings reached $2.50 billion. Considering the 583 million shares outstanding, our EPS moved to $4.28.

Applying an unchanged P/E of 10x (aligned with its 5-year historical average—Fig. 6), our target price reached $42.8 with a 14% upside potential. We should consider the negative one-off nature of the higher operating costs and $15 million impact from interest rates, which are linked to a mismatch between funding and royalties. Our upside also does not consider:

  1. With a $2 billion buyback, the company might redeem at least 9.2% of the current outstanding shares.
  2. "That other royalty companies usually trade at a premium compared to their respective sector."

Fig 6

Risks

Downside risks include 1) worse than anticipated performance of current royalties, 2) new corporate tax, 3) higher interest rates, 4) value destruction in the royalties deal, 5) higher comps in the royalty investing space, and 6) lower synergies to RP Management. Still related to our negative note on Vertex's potential arbitration results, we report the following update from RP's press release (Fig. 7). This news could mark a negative precedent in Royalty Pharma’s history, potentially resulting in higher legal costs ahead.

Fig 7

Conclusion

With a 14% upside to our one‑year target, supported by a buyback program and a coupon yield above 6% annually, we remain buyers. Our target price already factors in one‑off downside more than it does in future upside opportunities.

data.title: AIO: Trades At A Premium Valuation Despite Flaws
data.description:
Overview

I've always been fascinated by funds that can provide exposure to some of the most innovative companies in the world, while simultaneously providing investors with a high level of income. Virtus Artificial Intelligence & Technology Opportunities Fund (AIO) strikes a balance between growth and income, and I believe that the efficient portfolio strategy makes this fund a great long-term holding. I previously covered AIO and issued a buy rating due to the portfolio strategy, strong performance, and healthy dividend coverage. I wanted to revisit the fund to assess its most updated annual report and provide insights into its valuation.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, AIO's share price has increased by about 24.6%. The fund experienced a large decline in the first quarter of the year alongside the rest of the market, but thankfully AIO was capable of riding the recovery momentum over recent months. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return increases to 34.3% over the same period. AIO is interesting because it provides investors with a generous starting dividend yield of 7.3%.

However, the fund still trades at a slight premium to NAV, and there are some risks that investors should be aware of. For instance, AIO is likely to underperform more traditional index-tracking ETFs that can provide a greater level of capital appreciation. Additionally, AIO's level of net investment income has been extremely weak in recent years. The fund offers very little downside protection, and the distributions may not be sustainable during periods where market indexes are moving sideways or declining. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy that AIO implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the most recent fact sheet, AIO has total managed assets of $941M that are spread across 96 different positions. The fund aims to focus on holdings that are embracing disruptive AI innovations and technologies, while also including exposure to relevant hardware companies. However, AIO takes a blended exposure approach by including some income securities alongside traditional common equities. The breakdown is as follows:

  • Common Equities: 52.19%
  • Convertible Securities: 29.31%
  • High Yield Bonds: 15.69%
  • Cash and equivalents: 2.81%

Looking at the sector allocation of the fund's equities, we can see that AIO is primarily focused on Information Technology companies, which make up 45.44% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Financials and Consumer Discretionary investments, which account for 11.51% and 9.31% of assets, respectively. However, investors should anticipate that AIO's technology holdings will have the greatest impact on the daily price change and total return potential.

The fund provides exposure to many high-quality large-cap companies. According to the top holdings, Nvidia (NVDA) is the largest holding at 5.13%. This is followed by exposure to other large-cap holdings like Meta Platforms (META), Microsoft (MSFT), Oracle (ORCL), Flex (FLEX), and JPMorgan (JPM) just to name a few. However, we must remember that AIO also has exposure to fixed-income securities within, so the fund may not fully participate in the growth of the market.

Updated Valuation

At the time of my last coverage, AIO traded at a slight premium to NAV of 6.75%. Due to the recent market uncertainty, AIO's premium to NAV slightly declined to approximately 3.45%. However, the current premium valuation still sits well above AIO's average price to NAV discount of 6.1% over the last three-year period. In an effort to help visualize AIO's current valuation, I added the red line in the graph below. As we can see, AIO's current valuation sits well above its average range since the fund's inception.

In an effort to determine whether or not the premium valuation is justified, I reviewed the most updated annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.01 per share, while net realized gains were $4.70 per share. Looking back on prior years, net investment income levels were consistently low. The low net investment income levels may be attributed to the primary focus on technology holdings, but I also hope to see major improvements here since the fund also has exposure to income producing convertible securities. This poor performance in terms of net investment income has caused AIO to be very reliant on net realized gains to fuel NAV growth and support distributions.

The only issue with this dependency on net realized gains is that AIO offers no protection during market declines. As you can imagine, it can be difficult for a fund to capture net realized gains during periods where market indexes are declining. For instance, AIO recognized a large net realized loss in 2023, due to the market decline experienced throughout 2022. A higher level of net investment income may make the fund more resilient during market declines and help protect the NAV. Thankfully, AIO experienced a strong year of growth during 2024, which helped the NAV increase at the close of this report. The year-end NAV amounts are as follows:

  • 2020: $19.89 per share
  • 2021: $29.20 per share
  • 2022: $24.18 per share
  • 2023: $19.92 per share
  • 2024: $20.61 per share
  • 2025: $23.52 per share

Since the fund doesn't produce a meaningful amount of income, it essentially means that AIO is selling off its upside in order to prioritize distributions. Therefore, I want to put emphasis on the fact that AIO is best utilized by the investors that specifically want to focus on income. If you are an investor that wants to pursue the highest possible total return within your portfolio, you will likely be better off with a more traditional index-tracking ETF.

Risk Profile

Since AIO needs to sell off portions of its securities as a way to fuel growth. The fund is essentially sacrificing price appreciation for income generation. Therefore, I believe that investors with a longer time horizon ahead may be better off with a more traditional approach, such as Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ). To demonstrate AIO's weakness, I pulled in a total return comparison of AIO versus QQQ, since AIO's inception. As we can see below, QQQ outpaces AIO in total return, despite AIO offering a much higher starting dividend yield of 7.3%.

I am very bullish on the outlook of the technology sector due to the evolving nature of different artificial intelligence products and services. I already own a pair of META's AI glasses, frequently use Google's (GOOGL) (GOOG) Gemini LLM, and continue to see different use cases for varying forms of AI. I strongly believe that AI will continue to be a large growth driver for businesses across the world, as they evolve how we work, interact with different forms of entertainment, and influence the tools we use for daily life.

Despite AIO providing exposure to the very same companies that have some form of exposure to the AI market, the fund is not a great choice for participating in the AI expansion. For instance, Precedence Research estimates that the size of the AI market can increase at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 19.20% through 2034. Since AIO will limit the level of capital appreciation experienced, investors may be leaving money on the table by prioritizing income instead of growth.

Lastly, the fund implements leverage as a part of its operating strategy, and this can increase the risk profile. The use of leverage is typically used as a way to amplify returns when an attractive opportunity is present. The amount of leverage being used sits around 13.62% of assets, which is equivalent to $130M. I consider this to be a moderate amount of leverage, but there may still be some negative consequences. For instance, investors must consider the risk that leverage is allocated to investments that don't perform as expected, which can actually amplify losses instead.

Dividend Sustainability

According to the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.15 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.3%. If we take this monthly payout and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $1.80 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, AIO has an inconsistent history of being able to support payouts. Just for reference, here are the year-end earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: $0.22 per share
  • 2021: $11.80 per share
  • 2022: ($0.17) per share
  • 2023: ($2.46) per share
  • 2024: $2.49 per share
  • 2025: $4.71 per share

As we can see, AIO has been able to generate earnings that outpace the distribution amount in three of the last five years. While AIO has no issue generating positive earnings during periods where market indexes are trending upward, I am cautious about the fund's sustainability during prolonged periods of decline. For instance, let's suppose that market indexes remain flat for the next two years as a result of tariffs. As it stands, AIO's usual net investment income levels aren't sufficient enough to support payouts on their own, and if market indexes aren't increasing, AIO may not be able to generate enough gains to support NAV growth and distributions.

However, I wouldn't necessarily worry yet. AIO has been able to produce large enough earnings on its positive years, that can support multiple years of distributions. For instance, 2025's total earnings of $4.71 per share are enough to support an annual distribution of $1.8 per share for more than two and a half years of time. Therefore, AIO is likely to keep payouts the same at this time, and I wouldn't anticipate a cut.

Additionally, the fund seems to prioritize making distributions in a tax-efficient manner. For instance, the most recent Section 19(a) notice indicates that 89.4% of the distributions YTD have been paid out using long-term capital gains, which have favorable tax consequences for investors. Assuming the fund can maintain payouts with a similar breakdown, AIO can be a great choice for retirees that want to collect a high rate of income without worrying about the tax burden.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my hold rating on AIO at this time. I like the fund's structure and strategy, but there are clear areas that can be improved. For instance, the level of net investment income needs to be improved, especially for a fund that puts a clear emphasis on income generation. The weak net investment income levels make AIO reliant on net realized gains, which makes the fund more vulnerable to market declines. Additionally, the price still trades at a premium valuation in relation to its usual range over prior years. The focus on income generation also means that AIO is likely to underperform more straight-forward ETFs like QQQ over the long term.

data.title: IEF: Staying Bullish As Spread Repricing Echoes Opportunity
data.description:
The iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (NASDAQ:IEF) is back in focus today. We last covered the fund in May, reiterating our bullish outlook on the basis of U.S. Treasury yields presenting solid return opportunities. Since then, yields have dropped to circa 420 - 425 basis points, IEF has generated a total return of around 3%. However, additional material factors have emerged, allowing us to re-engage with the IEF; herewith is a summary of our updated thoughts.

Real Macro Variables (A Cyclical Outlook)

Let's re-engage with a few real macro variables to set a base for our cyclical outlook. The following diagram communicates a few critical points; a discussion follows.

Variable Output
Quarterly Real GDP growth 3%
Output Gap 13.33%
Annual PCE Change 2.6%
CPI Y/Y 2.7%
Consumer Sentiment 60.7
JOLTS 7.437M
Unemployment Rate 4.25%
Non-Farm Payrolls +73K

Source: Trading Economics, Investing.com, YCharts - Periodicity may differ among variables

The U.S.' GDP grew at an annualised rate of 3% in Q2 after slumping by -0.5% in Q2. We don't think these figures are particularly informative. Most of Q1's slowing seemingly resulted from a surge in tariff-driven imports, which depleted the net exports line item in the GDP calculation. The opposite occurred in Q2, allowing real GDP to settle higher – We think looking at sub-variables as determinants of the U.S.'s cyclical growth outlook would be more beneficial than observing headline GDP figures.

The primary factors we've decided to track include: the labor market, PCE, and consumer sentiment. In our view, cyclical growth will mostly depend on consumer sentiment and business' confidence to invest. Moreover, we think Federal Reserve decisions will have a significant impact on sentiment, and therefore, we deem labor market and inflationary data critical.

As matters stand, consumers have lost confidence, which we anticipated to occur a while back, largely due to cyclical economic effects (the output gap it arguably overcooked), a higher-for-longer interest rate environment, and the loss in trade confidence amid an unstable foreign policy environment. Recent data releases have aligned with a view we've echoed on Seeking Alpha for numerous months. For example, job openings data remains in a downtrend, labor force participation is stuck below pre-COVID levels, non-farm payrolls missed forecasts (yes, I'm aware of data quality concerns), and consumer sentiment is under pressure. Reported PCE and CPI have both trended higher post-liberation day; however, we see the variables being sticky – short-term price hikes -> short-to-intermediate-term impact on consumers -> substitution emerges and lower consumer sentiment consolidates -> limitations imposed on companies' ability to pass input costs through to their end markets. Unfortunately, the Federal Reserve has been placed in a position where inflation uncertainty restricts its base case for target rate cuts. Considering all these variables, we see the following pathway unfolding: growth slows organically, the Federal Reserve acts slower-than anticipated, growth and inflation both taper to their lower boundaries, leading the bond market into pricing a series of rate cuts throughout 2026. Additionally, we see investors turning risk-off, providing the treasury market with additional support.

To close this section, I'd like to add a few additional notes on inflation:

I want to mention two additional matters regarding inflation. First, many have referred to tariffs lately while ignoring oil prices. WTI has settled in the mid-$60 range and its curve is in backwardation, a significant improvement from the $120 peak in 2022. Secondly, it's worth noting that the Fed emphasizes PCE, not just CPI – PCE accounts for substitution, making it a critical indicator at this time.

Curve and Spreads Analysis

The short-to-intermediate part of the U.S. Treasury Yield curve has continued to level down in recent months, ignoring temporary increases in CPI and PCE. In isolation, the short-to-intermediate part of the curve anticipates lower future interest rates, likely due to a slower growth outlook, softer inflation, or anomalous factors. In contrast, the longer-end of the curve has stayed elevated; whether due to re-growth expectations, a higher fiscal risk premium, or anomalous factors, remains debatable.

The spread between the 10-year nominal yield and the 3-month nominal yield acts as a useful barometer for cyclical measures. The spread has consolidated around the zero midpoint in recent weeks and within the +/- 25 basis point boundary in recent months. Conventionally, the consolidated level would signal mild potential for a recession. In our view, the current consolidated level signals a cyclical slowdown as opposed to a hard landing; a sustained level below -50 basis points would likely confirm a strong base case for a pending recession.

Though a useful cyclical tool, the 10Y-2Y signals policy pathways more than cyclical outlooks. The 10Y-2Y has consolidated around the 50 basis point level in recent months. In our view, the level suggests policy uncertainty, rather than clear conviction on Fed rate target ranges, most likely due to the unknowns introduced by a fiscal regime change, a monetary regime change, and the, AI as a productivity growth variable, debate. Alternatively, we could compare the 2Y-3M spread and speculate that the market believes the Fed will hold rates strong, leading to diminished cyclical growth – this comparison in itself can predict a recession but it's probably less meticulous than the 10Y-3M.

In our opinion, sustained signs of a cyclical slowdown and policy uncertainty would provide support to IEF ETF, which we largely base on a risk-off narrative. Keep in mind that, as a Treasury vehicle, IEF is priced in advance, meaning, barring autocorrelation, IEF is priced based on where the market believes future nominal rates will settle; it's not priced contemporaneously. So, even if the Fed keeps rates high and even if PCE remains sticky, we argue that future rates need to be isolated.

Real Rates, Breakevens, and The Term Premium

Real rates have struggled to find direction. Breakevens have found calm but remain susceptible to tariff noise. Keep in mind that breakevens price inflation uncertainty, not just the price level. Our view is that real rates will settle slightly lower in the coming quarters, which we base on a lower neutral rate (here's a link leading to a study of neutral rates by PIMCO), calming of the fiscal risk premium, and investor risk-off preference buffering against a potential productivity drag. Similarly, we think breakevens will taper slightly as the inflationary impact of tariffs becomes clearer. In addition, as argued before, we think the Fed's current hold on rates and the softening demand side of the economy will lead to the market pricing lower ex-ante inflation.

I wanted to isolate a study of the term premium to provide context on duration. The term premium spiked since late 2024. Some might suggest that the spike has resulted from re-growth expectations (after Fed target rates dropped), others may argue that the higher premium is a result of fiscal risks. We aren't quite sure about the term premium's year-to-date attribution. However, by eyeballing it, we'd say that there was an overreaction on regrowth. Moreover, we think the fiscal premium is justified, but many of the factors mentioned in the Moody's (MCO) downgrade report were likely already priced in. As for now, we remain concerned about issuances—the U.S. is issuing quite rampantly, which could drive term premiums higher throughout the curve, given that the U.S. already possesses an elevated debt level. That being said, we think the term premium will consolidate in the 40s if it receives a kicker from risk-off preference and once media noise about U.S. fiscal pressures fade.

As a judgement call, we'd price our six-month nominal 10-year yield target between a range of 390 and 410 basis points with a preference for the lower end. If realized, we could see IEF deliver valuable price returns–we probably won't see it sacrificing much yield, given that its portfolio's turnover (into new lower-yielding securities) would likely lag.

IEF's Key Metrics Revisited

IEF's metrics have altered by a slight amount since our last coverage. For example, its 30-day SEC yield has shrunk from 4.18% to 4.10%. Moreover, its average yield-to-maturity has decreased to 4.09% from the previous 4.19% we reported in May. IEF's duration and convexity metrics have stayed largely unchanged, but its option-adjusted spread has risen to 2.23 basis points from the 1.62 basis points conveyed in May.

In terms of distributions. Remember, IEF distributes its interest income. To our observation, IEF hasn't distributed capital gains nor returns of capital. The vehicle's return structure conveys its distribution profile, showing no signs of value destruction while simultaneously communicating a tendency to distribute its interest income instead of reinvesting the proceeds.

Risks Worth Considering

Credit Risk Being Assigned To U.S. Sovereigns

You might've noticed that IEF's option-adjusted spread has picked up to 2.23 basis points from 1.62 basis points since our latest coverage. The change could be due to portfolio composition. However, systematic influence wouldn't be a coincidence. The U.S. 10-year CDS spread has drifted structurally higher in recent years, most likely due to increased debt levels, fiscal dominance, insurance activity (CDS insurance contracts), and higher interest expenses. Since April 2023, the 10-year CDS has reached the 50 level on three occasions—we could see investors price credit risk into the U.S.' sovereigns if current CDS levels persist, in turn, influencing IEF's discount rates.

Fiscal Dominance

The U.S. is under fiscal dominance, whereby the government's spending focus has threatened the Federal Reserve's independence. Although the Fed has remained steadfast in its goals to drag inflation back to a region of stability, the lingering threats of fiscal dominance might add an unwanted risk premium to U.S. Treasuries and IEF alike.

Media Noise

Anecdotally, we think the media has made quite a lot of noise by reiterating the U.S.' fiscal concerns and the Trump administration's policy shifts. We think most of the media's concerns refer to known factors; astute bond investors probably priced fiscal strain, Japan funding costs, and President Trump's restrictive trade and relaxed tax policy beliefs in advance. Sure, the scale of tariff policies came as a shock; however, even that seems to be old news.

Having said that, the media's persistent focus on negative issues could lead some to divest from U.S. Treasuries, and in turn, devalue IEF.

Our Outlook May Simply Be Erroneous

Our outlook is merely our own. Interest rates are highly speculative by nature, and therefore we acknowledge the possibility of being wrong.

IEF Isn't A Hold-to-Maturity Asset

IEF isn't a direct held-to-maturity investment. It sells and buys treasuries to maintain a specific duration target. As such, the ETF's advertised yields may never materialize. In our view, IEF remains a cyclical trade as opposed to a long-term investment opportunity.

Our IEF Total Return Outlook (Six Months)

IEF has delivered a year-to-date total return of roughly 5.95%, which is well-beyond its since-inception annualised return of 3.48%. Based on our analysis, we expect the ETF to deliver excess returns by year-end. In our six-month outlook, we think IEF could deliver an additional 1.50%–2.00% in price returns and 1.6%–1.8% in distribution income; we range that to a potential return of between 3.00% and 3.50% (not by simple addition).

Herewith is a brief explanation of our return outlook.

  • As discussed earlier in the article, we expect the 10-year yield to sacrifice another 20–30 basis points within the next six months. By multiplying the ETF's current duration by 25 basis points, we derive a return target of 1.75%. We set the range at 1.50%-2.00% to remain conservative. Moreover, we wanted to account for the ETF's sub-10-year duration and potential portfolio composition adjustments.
  • The distribution return component was calculated by adjusting IEF's current weighted average coupon to a semi-annual figure, whereby a judgement call was made to account for fluctuations in IEF's portfolio composition.
  • Adding the higher ends (or lower ends) of the two variables together to establish a lower (or upper) boundary would be erroneous as yield and price adjustments aren't symmetrical. Therefore, we compressed our target range to account for the odd relationship between yield interest rate sensitivity and price-based interest rate sensitivity.

We are quite confident about our outlook. However, note that this sub-section provides an informal judgement call instead of a formal return model. Moreover, as previously mentioned, our rate outlook is speculative, it doesn't provide certainty; IEF's max drawdown of 23.92% illustrates what can happen when rate speculation goes wrong.

Conclusion

We hold the view that iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF's run is far from over. The ETF has delivered slid returns since the turn of the year, and we think additional total returns of between 3.00% and 3.50% (for the next six months) are on the cards. Our basis primarily derives from our 10-year yield target, which is nearer to the 400 basis point level. Moreover, we think IEF's portfolio will sustain its off-the-run distribution yield for an adequate amount of time to see it benefit from price returns without sacrificing much yield.

Although risks such as persistent fiscal dominance, IEF's cyclicality, and inflationary uncertainty remain, we are net bullish for the time being.

Consensus: Bullish

data.title: Mogo Inc. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Mogo Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Blue Owl Capital: Shareholder Dilution Makes It A Hold
data.description:
Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL) had a nice run in January up to and shortly after Trump's inauguration on Jan 20th. However, after the all time high on Jan 23rd, the stock has had a run to forget. Note that while the broader market has been on a record setting spree, Blue Owl Capital remains below its pre-Liberation Day levels. 2025 Q2 earnings show that the company's fundamentals are solid. Strong FRE metrics and AUM show that the asset manager is healthy. However, Class A shares saw a notable increase, and so shareholders have been diluted even though that's partially because of their acquisitions. As a result, adjusted per share metrics were only decent. The forward P/E is currently at a major premium to the Financials sector and so after considering the dilution, I would say the valuation is only fair at best. Therefore, I am initiating the stock at a hold rating.

Equity Fundraising Surges

Before we move into a discussion of how the company is performing financial wise, I believe it is worth mentioning briefly their equity fundraising stats as it's a clear demonstration of the demand for their investment services. Firstly, the average funds raised per quarter from 2021 Q2 to 2023 Q2 were $4.3 billion. Since then, the average per quarter has risen to $6.5 billion, showing that the company is successfully attracting new capital at a strong pace. They stated that "significant growth [was] driven by differentiated products, a broadening of [their] product offering, and the scaling of [their] distribution platforms." Furthermore, 2025 Q2's $12.1 billion was a major step up from the prior year period's $5.4 billion raised and also from the previous quarter's $6.7 billion. In my view, these are strong signs of a healthy asset manager.

Resilient Revenue Growth

Blue Owl Capital's top line saw resilient growth for 2025 Q2. They reported $703.106 million of GAAP revenues, up 27.87% YoY. While there was a bit of deceleration from Q1's 33% increase, a growth rate in the high 20s percentage range really cannot be complained about. The main driver of these resilient results was management fees, which saw a 34% increase to $623.369 million. Performance revenues surged 421%, but their contribution to total revenues is barely noteworthy.

Fee Related Earnings

In non-GAAP terms, Blue Owl Capital also saw robust results overall. For FRE Management Fees, they saw a 30% increase to $620.152 million for Q2, while for the LTM, they saw an even stronger 32% increase to $2.319 billion. With this being by far the largest contributor to FRE revenues, these strong metrics indicate that the core business is solid. In terms of expenses, for the quarter, they saw a 30% increase, and for the LTM, they saw a 36% increase. In both timeframes, FRE General, Administrative and Other Expenses led the way in terms of increases, and so there was a notable decrease in corporate efficiency. As a result, fee-related earnings increased just 21% and 23% for Q2 and the LTM, respectively.

Do note that in adjusted terms, FRE per share only increased 9.52% and distributable earnings per share only increased by 10.53% YoY. On their balance sheet, you can see that total stockholders' equity attributable to Blue Owl Capital increased by 10.48% since the end of 2024. In addition, in their consolidated statements of changes in stockholders' equity, you can see that Class A shares increased from 54 million to 65 million YoY as a clear signal of dilution. While some of this can be traced to acquisitions, shareholders were nonetheless adversely affected. FRE and distributable earnings growth were respectable for the company as a whole, but existing shareholders did see quite a bit of that diluted away.

Assets Under Management Soar

As you can see in the above diagram, all three categories of Assets Under Management increased significantly from the prior year period. For total AUM, they hit $284.1 billion after a 48% increase. They cited "the Kuvare, IPI, and Atalaya acquisitions, as well as capital raised and appreciation across the business," as reasons for this strength. This is another indication that they are successfully attracting new capital but also that their investment performance has been strong. For fee-paying AUM, they saw a growth rate of 46%, and this increase was attributed to similar reasons as the total AUM increase. Perhaps the most encouraging of all is their Permanent Capital, however. They saw a 41% increase, and while that is the lowest of the three growth rates discussed here, they stated that "permanent capital generated 87% of management fees during the last twelve months." The fact that this capital is permanent means that Blue Owl Capital can invest these funds for the long term and seek returns for investors that aren't possible if withdrawals are continuously made.

Valuation Is Acceptable

Throughout today's analysis, it was shown that Blue Owl's fundamentals are strong. Top line results were strong, and non-GAAP FRE metrics were also robust. However, as discussed earlier, in adjusted per share terms, earnings growth was quite sluggish. This was mainly due to shareholder dilution. Even though some of that was due to acquisitions, such as the Prima acquisition, this is still a potential deterrent, as the strong fundamentals may not be translating towards actual shareholder returns.

In the above chart, Blue Owl Capital's forward P/E ratio is shown. The multiple has contracted from a major high early this year, and after a modest rebound it finds itself at 23.50. The Financials sector has a median GAAP and non-GAAP forward P/E ratio of 11.4 and 10.72, respectively, as of this writing. Either way, Blue Owl is at a major premium. When just looking at the company-wide results, I believe the strong metrics in FRE and AUM are able to justify this valuation gap. However, after considering the dilution factor, I would say that the valuation is only decent and maybe slightly rich since shareholders could experience a somewhat muted effect of this fundamental strength. Therefore, I would be hesitant to call the stock a buy even though the company as a whole is performing in a very strong manner.

Technicals Are Mixed

For the long term, the 50-day SMA broke below the 200 day SMA back in April for a death cross. Obviously, that is an indication of significant bearishness. Since around mid-June, the deviation between the SMAs has decreased a bit, and so downward momentum isn't all that strong. Recently, the stock broke beneath the 50-day SMA but has been able to recover as a sign that the long-term bulls are still resilient. As for the near term, there are some mixed signals. For the Bollinger Bands, the stock continues to sit below the midline on the current trading day, and so there may still be some bearishness. The MACD echoes that as the indicator line remains below the signal line. However, the histogram has turned green for the past three bars, and so near-term downward momentum is receding. Furthermore, the RSI has fought back to a reading of 50.17, which means that the bulls are back in control even if it's just by a very thin margin. As a whole, I would say both the long- and near-term technical outlooks are mixed, as the stock still seems undecided on its next move.

In early April, the stock set a major low. However, there were signs that the bottom was in. Looking at the MACD and RSI indicators, you can see that their April lows held above their March lows. That is positive divergence, and so there was a good chance of a bullish reversal. The stock did bounce shortly after and so despite the technicals being mixed currently as discussed above, the odds are still likely favoring further upside from here.

Conclusion

For Blue Owl Capital the company, things are headed in the right direction. Revenues are growing healthily, fee-related earnings are robust and assets under management have swelled. For shareholders, this is still very encouraging but the dilution, as mentioned many times, is a potential concern as it erodes returns at the end of the day. The valuation is quite high and so while the fundamentals are strong enough to justify the forward P/E ratio, the dilution makes the stock's price tag not very attractive currently. Don't get me wrong, the company seems to be an excellent asset manager but I would wait until dilution concerns fade before buying. Therefore, I have decided to initiate Blue Owl Capital at a hold rating.

data.title: CIBR: Growth Prospects, Sector Tailwinds Make It A Long-Term Bet
data.description:
With every data going online, there exists a need to protect it. Now more than ever, the necessity that is cybersecurity comes into the spotlight. Cybersecurity is quoted as a key concern for every enterprise gearing up for the future. Just to set the context, the average cost of a data breach, according to a report by IBM in 2024, is $4.4M and 73% of all organizations have been the victims of a ransomware attack. What is more staggering is that global cybercrime is projected to cost 10.5 trillion USD annually by 2025, nearly 10% of global GDP. With that thought in mind, we would like to present a compelling long-term investment opportunity for this field: First Trust NASDAQ Cybersecurity ETF (NASDAQ:CIBR).

Cybersecurity is Still Not a Ripe Market

Cybersecurity has been one of the best performing technology themes in the last few years, expecting to top $213B in spending in 2025, up from $193B in 2024, according to Gartner, Inc. The trend is expected to continue, with the total amount spent projected to be $240B in 2026. International Data Corporation (IDC) is similarly optimistic, expecting cybersecurity spending to reach $377B USD in 2028, with the U.S. and Western Europe expected to account for more than 70% of global security spending in 2025.

Security Software is expected to be the fastest growing segment as cloud-based systems are becoming more popular, according to Gartner.

Cybersecurity, in my opinion, represents one of the most attractive investment themes long term and according to Gartner, higher defense budgets, rising threats, increasing regulatory pressure, and better cybersecurity awareness will uplift cybersecurity spending in the medium to long term. In the short term, rising threats and expanding use of AI will remain key catalysts.

What we would like to note at this point is that, even though cybersecurity is an already established market, it accounts for only around 4% of overall projected global IT spending for 2025. We think this figure will increase, considering the threats AI and boiling geopolitics brings. A traction in this regard is shown in the latest CIO Survey conducted by Citi, showcasing that cybersecurity is once again top priority for CIOs with budgets for cybersecurity rising to 6.1% from 5.3% last quarter.

Cybersecurity Powered by AI

According to one research report, spending for AI enabled cybersecurity products is set to amount to around $134B, growing at an impressive CAGR of nearly 28% for the forecasted period. If materialized it would present a major potential upside for cybersecurity firms, especially if we take into consideration the high margin expected for these types of products.

CrowdStrike (CRWD) is a pioneer in this regard, launching Charlotte AI, an agentic generative AI assistant intended for everyday tasks such as triaging alerts and investigating incidents. The results are impressive, exhibiting 98% decision accuracy with agentic detection triage and saving on average 40 hours a week by automating detection triage. Forti AI is another example of AI usage. Launched by Fortinet (FTNT), it uses AI to enhance detection and response to emerging threats, automating security operations while maintaining top-notch user interaction.

The Fund

First Trust NASDAQ Cybersecurity ETF is an ETF designed to track the performance of the Nasdaq CTA Cybersecurity Index TM with full replication strategy. The fund is intended to invest at least 90% of its net assets in the constituents of the index, while the index is comprised of companies in different supply chain points of the cybersecurity industry, primarily companies involved in building, implementing, and management of security protocols regarding private, public networks, computers, and mobile devices. In order to be included in the index, a company must have a market cap of at least $500M, an average 3M daily traded value of at least $1M and a minimum of 20% free float. Weighting is set at a maximum constituent weight of 8% and a minimum weight of 0.25%. Regarding expenses, the fund has a Seeking Alpha C Expense grade, mainly due to the relatively higher expense ratio of 0.59%, but not uncommon among thematic ETFs. The bid/ask spread is relatively low, with a bid/ask % spread of 0.07%.

What is typical is the generally concentrated top 10 exposure, with around 60% of total assets being in the top 10 holdings. From a sector perspective, naturally around 93.5% of the fund's assets are in the technology sector, with the rest being Industrials.

Regarding risk metrics, the fund has a relatively higher standard deviation than the median ETF, is more concentrated, and is exhibiting a higher tracking error. What we like about this ETF is the lower Beta of 0.75 and a higher Alpha of around 7.48.

Exhibiting superior performance relative to peers on a 5-year basis, the fund has achieved a cumulative price return of around 97%.

Summarily graded through the Seeking Alpha system of ETF grades, the fund bodes well compared to other peer funds:

CIBR is lagging in grading in groups such as Momentum compared to Amplify Cybersecurity ETF (HACK) and SPDR® S&P Kensho Future Security ETF (FITE), slightly underperforming on a YTD basis. The expense ratio being 0.59% is higher than the expense ratios of FITE, IHAK and Global X Cybersecurity ETF (BUG), but heavily proving to be worth it so far.

In terms of liquidity, boasting an AUM of nearly $10B and the highest liquidity with nearly 1M average daily share volume, CIBR is the flagship fund for the cybersecurity industry.

As noted earlier and instructed by the index policy, the fund is concentrated with 39 holdings and 61% of assets in the top 10 holdings. The turnover of 39% is relatively high but ups the chance for alpha generation. The HHI Index of the fund is 522.

The dividend yield of the fund is the highest of the group both on a TTM basis and as a 4-year average, with the dividend paid quarterly.

Valuation

The fund is currently trading at a P/E of 28.14x, at a premium to the market multiple of 26.9x. We think the current premium is justified, as the cybersecurity market is expected to grow at an increased CAGR in the next couple of years, and cost savings and productivity gains are also a key factor in the valuation and will facilitate earnings expansion in the coming years. Relative to the 10Y median of 27.3x, the current level is also a slight premium, i.e. the fund is trading at the upper band of the historical range.

The top 10 companies in the fund are presented below:

Summarized as a group, they are characterized by high revenue growth, high margins and ROE, with elevated P/E multiples.

From a technical standpoint, the fund is broke below the 20D and 50D MA, signaling a downward trajectory with support at the 200D MA.

Risks

Short-term, the risk remains of a global slowdown hampering IT and cybersecurity spending, we think the chance for a strong pullback in cybersecurity spending is low. Medium-to-long term, the large public cloud vendors to which much of the market is exposed to, have their own cybersecurity products. This poses risks to further growth for the traditional cybersecurity companies such as Palo Alto and CrowdStrike. Regarding operating risks, there is a global shortage of IT specialists in the field of cybersecurity, with the World Economic Forum estimating that figure to be around 4 million cyber professionals. While the companies in this ETF are large and are less prone to such risks, the current shortage of a skilled workforce could hamper industry growth. On the other hand, the global lack of cybersecurity professionals presents an opportunity for these firms to market their own solutions to small and medium enterprises.

Conclusion

Cybersecurity remains an important theme in the technological future we are all experiencing. AI has been a major tailwind in unveiling the need for companies to invest in cybersecurity, while also presenting product opportunity. We think this theme poses an attractive long-term opportunity for investors, and CIBR is an adequate instrument for this purpose. Given elevated valuation and market volatility, we would be cautious in terms of short-term position building while optimistic on long-term potential.

data.title: Duolingo: The Green Owl Did It Again!
data.description:
Introduction

Duolingo (NASDAQ:DUOL) reported Q2 results, which were very well received by the investment community. As a user of the app, I never paid too much attention to the company itself, so I wanted to take a look at the numbers and give some comments on the outlook and where I stand regarding the app and the company.

By the Numbers

Starting from the top, revenue came in at $252.3m, up 41.5% y/y, beating analysts’ estimates by $11.52m. Looking at the revenue breakdown in more detail, subscriptions make up most of the revenues at 83.5%, with Other revenue segments, which include advertising, Duolingo English Test, and In-App Purchases, making up the rest. Subscriptions saw growth of around 46% y/y, while another standout in terms of percentage was Advertising, coming in at 55.4% y/y growth. But as you can see below, it makes up a relatively low percentage of total revenues.

In terms of profitability and efficiency, Q2 GAAP EPS came in at $0.91, which beat analysts’ estimates by a fantastic margin of 33 cents. Gross margins saw a slight decrease of 100bps to 72.4%, primarily due to lower subscription gross margin, which was driven by higher costs of GenAI related to the expansion of the company’s Max subscription tier. Nevertheless, that is still a very healthy gross margin. Also, the company expected a much worse decline of 300 bps previously, so it’s a win. The costs of the AI tools decreased significantly, leading to only a 100 bps decline overall. Operating margin came in at around 13.2%, which is up significantly from last year’s 10.5%. R&D and G&A expenses grew at a much slower pace than sales, leading to improvements in operating margins.

Regarding other key metrics, Daily Active Users grew 40% y/y, to 47.7m, paid subscribers increased 37% y/y to 10.9m, and total bookings rose 41% to $268m

What about the company’s financial situation? DUOL finished the year with around $1.1B in cash, equivalents, and short-term investments against zero debt. The little green owl is flush with cash and has no worries about the interest expense burden. It’s a fantastic position to be in, and one that allows the owl to use all that capital available to continue to innovate and attract more people going forward.

On top of these fantastic results and performance, Duo raised its full-year guidance to $1.01B-$1.02B, surpassing analysts’ estimates of $996.6m, and a major milestone of over $1B to be surpassed. The company also provided revenue estimates for Q3 to be in the range of $257m-$261m, well above analysts’ estimates of $253m.

It seems the company is firing on all cylinders and doesn’t seem to be stopping the momentum anytime soon. A double beat and a raise were very well received by the investing community, which rewarded the company with a massive share price surge of over 35% at the open, but since the markets are somewhat unstable due to some macroeconomic factors (Trump), the company gave back more than half of those gains, and is currently up around 12% on the day.

Overall, I agree with the investors here. This was another great quarter for the company, back-to-back; however, was the share price surge justified? There is hardly any company that reports earnings that deserves such swings in share price, up or down. The raised guidance is good, but it wasn’t raised by that much, in my opinion. I guess what really happened is people are looking for any glimmer of hope when it comes to the future performance that doesn’t signal a slowdown, and DUOL was right there to give it to them.

Comments on the Outlook

So, what can the owl do to maintain such momentum going forward? What are the key catalysts that will help it push to new highs every quarter?

DUOL’s CEO, Luis von Ahn, emphasized that the company is still in the early stages of its growth journey, and further investments in expanding AI functionalities will enhance educational outcomes and user engagement. The AI-powered video call conversation practice and personalized feedback tools were highlighted to be the key. So, essentially, the company is banking on Max subscriptions to do the heavy lifting.

I am currently on the annual Super subscription, which I got for around $30 as a Christmas offer back in December. That was rather cheap for me to get since I am in a country that is predominantly non-English speaking, and I thought learning the language on the app would complement my everyday life. I was quite disappointed to learn that there are two premium tiers, one being Super and the other Max. And of course, Max is the cool one and costs a lot more than Super. At more than three times the cost of super (if you get a deal), I don’t know if I would be willing to try it out only to get disappointing results in the end.

I seem to be not the only one since Max’s subscription is growing at a slower pace than Super’s. If they want to get Max to be the key growth driver, the price needs to come down quite a bit, and I’m sure it will since the costs of AI tools are already coming down. So, maybe in the future, there will only be one price tier, at a much more appealing pricing point than currently. $30 a month for some AI tools is a bit much in my opinion. The management said recently that they are looking to get the users to switch away from App Store purchases to direct payment flows, which should come as cheaper for all, not just users, but also increase the company’s efficiency and profitability.

The Super App

I do like the gamified look of the App, and I am excited to see that the app is slowly turning into a super app of sorts, offering not only language courses but other subjects like music, math, and, most recently, chess. Chess, particularly, has been highlighted as a fantastic growth story in the most recent quarter, surpassing math and music. There is a lot more growth seen in chess because it is still not available on Android phones. If we look at the company’s latest 10K, we can see that Apple processed 61.6% of total revenues, while Google processed around 24%, which means there is a potential of another 20%+ to be unlocked once Android is going to get some love.

Going back to the whole Super App, what’s stopping the company from implementing other subjects into the app? The gamification of subject learning is a lot more enticing than just video and PDF reading online. I wouldn’t be surprised if the company wants to continue to grow, the management will announce some coding courses in the future. Nothing has been confirmed at all, but it only seems like a natural progression. Many apps on the Google Play Store have successfully implemented Duolingo-style gamification and lessons to teach many different languages, such as JavaScript, Python, and HTML/CSS. So, go from learning a foreign language to another language that robots can understand. It’s a no-brainer.

The Virality of Duo the Owl

The company’s mascot owl, Duo, has been rising like a phoenix from the ashes recently, with quite a few viral social media marketing successes. The bird’s sometimes unhinged, personality-driven social media presence helped the company get a lot of clicks in 2025. One good example is the Viral “Death” and Resurrection campaign of the owl, which garnered a lot of attention from the masses, spiking the mascot mentions up 25,500%, with over 45,000 hashtag mentions in just two weeks.

The owl’s character is on the most popular social media platforms that the younger generations use these days, like TikTok, Twitter, and Instagram (META), shows the character being very playful, humorous, and persistent, as well as playful guilt-tripping for missing a day or two on learning a language, which is a rather successful way of tapping into behavioral psychology’s concept of negative reinforcement that encourages habit formation. I fell for it clearly, as I achieved well over 500 days at one point, because of the Owl’s sad, teary-eyed expressions in my notification bar.

The social media tactic taps into the meme culture very well, and it is one of the most cost-effective ways of gaining attention in this day and age. Is it going to be as successful as the Stanley Cup tumbler’s phenomenal social media viral success that turned this company from a $75m revenue company to $750m juggernaut in just 4 years? I don’t think so, but it is certainly not going to hurt it!

Closing Comments

I am a fan of the app, no doubt. A couple of months ago, I lost my 500-something day streak because I was very occupied, so since then, I have not been as active, but I plan to get back into it and see all the new features I may have missed. I am not a fan of the company’s shares at this point, however. I would like to see how the rest of the year turns out, because to me, it feels like the company is priced to perfection at this point, and anything other than stellar performance going forward will be met quite negatively.

The company is no stranger to seeing its share price go up or down 30%+ on the day of earnings, so I will be interested to see what happens for the rest of the year. How is the company going to keep up the momentum? For now, I am assigning it a hold rating and will be paying a lot more attention in the future.

data.title: ORIX: More Repurchases And Upward Guidance Revision Are Potential Catalysts (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
ORIX Corporation (IX) (8591.T) is now deserving of a rating upgrade to Buy. I have identified re-rating triggers associated with the business outlook and its stockholder returns.

The May 13, 2025 write-up detailed its mixed prospects across different time horizons, which justified my prior neutral view.

Spotlight On Above-Consensus Earnings And ROE Expansion

ORIX's 1QFY26 (YE Mar 31 2026) financials were released on Thursday, Aug 7 before trading hours.

In the first three months of the new fiscal year, its bottom-line rose 24% to ¥0.11T on YoY terms. This was equivalent to 28 percent of the full-year management guide.

The sell-side had predicted a more modest JPY 93B quarterly net income based on S&P Capital IQ data. It implies that ORIX registered a substantial +15% beat.

My mid-May article highlighted the firm's "goal to raise its ROE (Return On Equity) by +2.2 ppts (percentage points) in the next three years to 11 percent in FY28". It took a big step towards achieving that with the recent quarter's 10.4% ROE. This also translated into a YoY increase of 170 bps (basis points).

The standouts were ORIX's Real Estate [RE] and Environment & Energy (E&E) units.

RE Division's Industry And Operational Statistics

The Japanese travel sector has grown strongly post-pandemic, as seen with the "visitor arrival" and "air passenger" metrics presented in the chart above. A July 15 Nikkei Asia report also indicated that 1H2025 (calendar year) "foreign visitors to Japan" reached a historical peak.

It's understandable that the company's hospitality properties, mainly situated in Japan's Kansai, have done well. This drove the RE segment's operating profit up 26% YoY to ¥36B in the Apr-Jun period.

The other star for ORIX was E&E which benefited from portfolio reorganization.

The group's new power storage facility has been up and running since the end of last year. It's a wise decision to enter this space boasting solid growth prospects thanks to aging grids and the green energy revolution. Its Q1 analyst briefing commentary described the installation as among "the largest" of its kind in the nation.

On the other hand, it sold a subsidiary engaged in refuse collection operations. This illustrates how ORIX has changed the business mix to enhance its financial profile. E&E's 1QFY2026 EBIT and ROA (Return on Assets) were 18% and 5.2 ppts higher YoY, respectively.

I'm impressed with the enterprise's latest showing. It adopted "self-help" measures like new ventures and divestitures to boost Environment & Energy's performance. Separately, the Real Estate arm capitalized on external trends like the tourism boom in its home market.

Anticipating Positive Surprises In November

ORIX's second quarter numbers should be revealed in three months' time. I think there's a high probability of it announcing further cash distributions and an improved outlook then.

It has executed on repurchases at a faster-than-expected pace.

The firm has a ¥0.1 trillion buyback program that's effective for the May 19, 2025 to Mar 31, 2026 time frame. ORIX has already completed two-fifths of the plan by the end of last month. At the current rate, this could be done in full before end of '25.

Its E&E division recently monetized its interest in an Indian decarbonization business called "Greenko" in end-June for $1.3B.

ORIX noted at its 1st quarter results call that "JPY 100 billion" was decided as the right "size of buyback" previously as "of May" 2025. It indicated "there may be a certain (more) appropriate amount" at the time of "interim (2Q/1H) financial results."

Management's remarks suggest that the company could possibly look at additional buybacks beyond the initial 100B yen. This makes sense, as its capacity for stockholder returns has expanded meaningfully with the "Greenko" sale proceeds.

My crystal ball is saying that ORIX has the capacity to carry out a similarly-sized repurchase plan before this fiscal year-end, thanks to the huge divestment.

Also, ORIX mentioned that "our full-year earnings forecast is currently under review" at the 1Q meeting. I see an upward revision in Nov as a real possibility.

The group didn't amend its existing FY26 guide, despite a significant first quarter beat. That implied a modest JPY 28B rise this fiscal year, or 8% higher YoY. It's reasonable to make such a change after Q2 results with greater confidence, when half of the year has passed.

Its 2QFY26 net profit is expected to include a +¥93B accounting gain linked to the "Greenko" deal. This alone is equal to 87% of its bottom line for Q1.

In my May 13 piece, I drew attention to ORIX's planned purchase of a "controlling stake in (asset manager) Hilco Global" to "grow its group-wide AUM" and add "high-margin recurrent income". This M&A is targeted to be concluded by end-2QFY2026.

On top of that, Japan remains a popular tourist destination, which is great for RE. E&E might also conduct fresh capital recycling activities.

The factors mentioned above support an upgrade of its existing FY2026 net income guidance. I am estimating the revised figure to be ¥440B (+25%).

You Should Contemplate These Bear-Case Scenarios

ORIX could underperform in the event of a recession in Japan and other major economies. This will hurt its cyclical operations like hotels and also limit its ability to sell assets at good prices.

The company's future capital returns might disappoint its shareholders. If it intends to be more aggressive on investments, that will reduce the amount of cash available for distribution.

The Final Word

I've turned bullish on ORIX.

The favorable read-throughs from its recent quarterly results have led me to expect more buybacks and a guidance raise. These can act as catalysts to push up its share price.

My updated fair P/B valuation for ORIX is 1.23x as per the "Gordon Growth Model" implying a 26% upside (current: 0.98 times book). My ROE, Cost of Equity, Perpetuity Growth assumptions are 10.4% (1QFY26 figure), 9% (market average) and 3% (developed country's GDP expansion), respectively.
